pattern: Group corner points by hashing diagonal pairs: any two points with different x and y can serve as opposite corners, and a rectangle exists only when the other two corners are also present in the set.
hints: A rectangle with axis-aligned sides is fixed entirely by one of its diagonals, so think about pairs of points rather than quadruples.
Pick two points that differ in both coordinates as opposite corners; the remaining two corners are forced, so just check whether they exist.
Put every point in a hash set. For each pair (x1,y1),(x2,y2) with x1!=x2 and y1!=y2, see if (x1,y2) and (x2,y1) are present, and track the smallest |x1-x2|*|y1-y2|.

edge_inputs.description: one obvious unit square plus a stray interior point
two candidate rectangles sharing a side, the thinner one wins
no rectangle can be formed, must return zero
three corners present but the fourth missing
single point cannot form a rectangle
exact unit square gives area one
collinear points on one row never close a rectangle
two disjoint squares, the smaller area is chosen

stress.description: dense points on a small coordinate grid so many rectangles arise
stress.note: narrow grid maximises coincidental corner matches
